title: Aspect Ratio Calculator (preserve ratio when resizing)
shortDescription: Calculate width or height to preserve a target aspect ratio. Common ratios (16:9, 9:16, 1:1, 4:3, 21:9) one-click presets included.
description: Set a target ratio and one dimension; the other auto-fills. Reverse direction works the same way. Useful for video, photo cropping, and responsive web layout.
metaDescription: Free aspect ratio calculator. Preserve ratios like 16:9, 9:16, 1:1, 4:3 when resizing. Compute missing width or height instantly.

sections.heading: What aspect ratio is
paragraphs: Aspect ratio describes width relative to height: 16:9 means 16 units wide for every 9 tall. The same ratio works at any size — 1920×1080 and 800×450 are both 16:9. Preserving aspect ratio when resizing prevents stretched or squished images and is the basis of responsive design.

We accept whole numbers (16:9) or decimals (1.78:1) for the ratio, and any size for width/height. Edit any field — the others update to maintain the ratio. The decimal ratio (width / height) is shown for use in CSS calc() expressions or video editing tools.

sections.heading: Common ratios and where they're used
paragraphs: 16:9 — modern HDTV, computer monitors, YouTube, most films since 2009. The default for almost everything you do today.
9:16 — vertical video for Instagram Stories, TikTok, YouTube Shorts. The flip side of 16:9 for phone-first content.
1:1 — square. Instagram feed default until 2021. Album covers, profile photos.
4:3 — old TVs and computer monitors before HD. Still used for some classic-style content.
3:2 — DSLR camera default. 35mm film. Larger prints.
21:9 — ultrawide cinematic. Films shot anamorphically. Some PC monitors and LG TVs.

sections.heading: Why CSS aspect-ratio matters
paragraphs: Modern CSS has aspect-ratio: 16/9; — set the ratio and let the browser compute height from width (or vice versa). Avoids the old padding-bottom hack and is essential for responsive embeds and image placeholders to prevent layout shift.

Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) is a Core Web Vital. Embedded videos, images without dimensions, and ads can all cause CLS — fix by setting an explicit width/height (which the browser uses to compute aspect ratio) or using CSS aspect-ratio.

howTo.name: Pick a ratio
howTo.text: Tap a preset or enter custom W:H values.

howTo.name: Enter a known dimension
howTo.text: Either width or height — the other auto-fills.

howTo.name: Edit any field
howTo.text: Updates propagate to maintain the ratio.

faq.a: Same ratio, different notation. 16/9 = 1.778. The colon form is conventional in TV/web; the decimal form is conventional in cinema.

faq.q: How do I preserve aspect ratio in CSS?
faq.a: Use the aspect-ratio property: img { aspect-ratio: 16/9; width: 100%; height: auto; }. The image fills the container width and height adjusts to preserve the ratio.

faq.q: Why does my video look wrong on a 4:3 TV?
faq.a: Most modern videos are 16:9. On a 4:3 display, they either letterbox (black bars), pillar-box, or stretch. The TV's settings control which.
Yes — type 1.5 or 2.39. The calculator treats them as decimals; the simplified-ratio output only appears for integer ratios.

A separate concept: the shape of an individual pixel. Modern displays use square pixels (1:1). Some legacy SD video formats used non-square pixels (e.g., 1.0667 for NTSC widescreen). This calculator handles display aspect ratio, not pixel aspect ratio.
21:9 (more precisely 2.39:1 or 2.40:1) — wider than HDTV. Common in modern blockbusters. Streaming services often serve films in this ratio.
Because phones are typically held vertically. 9:16 fills the entire phone screen with content; 16:9 leaves bands at top and bottom on a vertical phone.
